name: Uber
overview:
Uber is a ride-sharing platform that connects riders with drivers. The system must track driver locations in
real-time, match ride requests to nearby drivers, compute accurate ETAs, handle dynamic pricing (surge), and
process payments. The system operates globally across hundreds of cities with different traffic patterns,
regulations, and demand profiles. It must handle millions of concurrent rides and hundreds of thousands of
active drivers.

inScope: Match ride requests to nearest available driver within 30 seconds
Compute and display ETA before and during ride
Dynamic surge pricing based on supply/demand

items: Support 5M concurrent rides, 500k active drivers globally
Ride matching decision within 30 seconds, matching computation <500ms
Eventual consistency for driver locations; strong consistency for ride assignment (no double-booking)
Horizontally scalable: handle 10x traffic spikes during peak hours or events

entities.name: Trip
entities.schema:
{
  trip_id: UUID,

  rider_id: string,
  driver_id: string,
  pickup: { lat, lng, address },
  dropoff: { lat, lng, address },
  status: enum(REQUESTED|MATCHED|DRIVER_EN_ROUTE|IN_PROGRESS|COMPLETED|CANCELLED),
  fare: decimal,
  surge_multiplier: float,
  requested_at: timestamp,
  completed_at: timestamp
}

approaches.title: Nearest Driver (Simple)
approaches.description: Find the geographically nearest available driver using Redis GEORADIUS. Doesn't consider driver heading, traffic, or driver rating. May match a driver who is technically close but will take 10 minutes due to traffic.
approaches.tradeoff: Suboptimal matching; ignores traffic and driver direction.

approaches.title: ETA-Based Matching
approaches.description: Compute ETA from each candidate driver to the pickup location. Match the driver with the lowest ETA. More accurate than distance-based matching. But computing ETA for 10 candidates requires 10 routing engine calls (~2 seconds).
approaches.tradeoff: ETA computation for all candidates adds latency; routing engine load.

approaches.title: Two-Phase: Geo Filter + ETA Scoring
approaches.description: Phase 1: Redis GEORADIUS to find top 10 candidates (fast, <5ms). Phase 2: Parallel ETA computation for all 10 candidates (~200ms). Match driver with lowest ETA. Driver assignment uses Redis distributed lock to prevent double-booking. If no driver found in 30 seconds, expand radius by 50% and retry.
approaches.tradeoff: Parallel ETA computation adds complexity; Redis lock contention under high load.

approaches.title: Fixed Per-Mile Rate
approaches.description: Fare = base_fare + per_mile_rate × distance. Doesn't account for traffic (same fare for 10-minute vs. 30-minute trip of same distance). Unfair to drivers stuck in traffic.
approaches.tradeoff: Unfair to drivers in traffic; doesn't reflect actual trip cost.

approaches.title: Time + Distance + Surge
approaches.description: Fare = base_fare + per_minute_rate × duration + per_mile_rate × distance + booking_fee, all multiplied by surge_multiplier. Accounts for both time and distance. Surge multiplier reflects supply/demand imbalance. Fare is estimated at request time (using ETA) and finalized at trip completion (using actual duration and distance).
approaches.tradeoff: Fare estimate may differ significantly from actual fare if traffic is worse than predicted.

components.name: Trip Service
components.responsibility: Manages trip lifecycle; validates requests; orchestrates matching

components.name: Driver Location Service
components.responsibility: Ingests driver GPS updates; maintains Redis Geo index

components.name: Matching Service
components.responsibility: Finds candidate drivers; computes ETAs; assigns with distributed lock

components.name: ETA Service
components.responsibility: Computes driver-to-pickup and pickup-to-dropoff ETAs using OSRM

components.name: Surge Pricing Service
components.responsibility: Computes surge multipliers per geohash zone every 60 seconds

components.name: Payment Service
components.responsibility: Processes fare payment on trip completion; handles refunds

components.name: Notification Service
components.responsibility: Sends push notifications to driver (new trip) and rider (driver matched)

components.name: Tracking Service
components.responsibility: Streams driver location to rider during active trip via WebSocket

components.name: Trip DB (PostgreSQL)
components.responsibility: Stores trip records; ACID transactions for fare processing

questions.title: 2. Driver Location at Scale
questions.content:
500k drivers × 1 update/4s = 125k writes/sec. The Driver Location Service uses a UDP-based protocol for
location updates (lower overhead than HTTP). Updates are batched in 100ms windows before writing to Redis
Geo. The Redis Geo index is sharded by geohash prefix across 20 nodes. Each node handles ~6,250
writes/sec — well within Redis's capacity.

questions.title: 3. Trip State Machine
questions.content:
Each trip goes through a well-defined state machine: REQUESTED → MATCHED → DRIVER_EN_ROUTE →
IN_PROGRESS → COMPLETED (or CANCELLED at any point). State transitions are atomic (PostgreSQL
transactions). Invalid transitions are rejected. The state machine ensures consistency: a trip cannot go from
REQUESTED to IN_PROGRESS without being MATCHED first.

questions.title: 4. Cancellation Handling
questions.content:
Riders can cancel before the driver arrives. Drivers can cancel before pickup. Cancellation policies: (1) Rider
cancels after driver is en route for >2 minutes: cancellation fee charged, (2) Driver cancels: driver is penalized
in matching score, rider is re-matched with priority. Cancellation events are published to Kafka for analytics and
driver behavior monitoring.

questions.title: 5. Fraud Detection
questions.content:
GPS spoofing (drivers faking their location) is a significant fraud vector. The system detects GPS spoofing by:
(1) Checking if the driver's reported speed is physically possible (>200 km/h is suspicious), (2) Comparing GPS
coordinates against the road network (driver shouldn't be in the middle of a lake), (3) Detecting sudden location
jumps (teleportation). Suspicious drivers are flagged for review.

keyFlows: ① Driver sends GPS every 4s → Driver Loc Svc → Redis Geo (GEOADD)
② Rider requests trip → Kafka → Matching Svc
③ Matching: GEORADIUS → top 10 → parallel ETA → assign (Redis lock)
④ Surge Pricing computes multiplier every 60s per geohash zone
⑤ Trip complete → Kafka → Payment Svc processes fare

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study Uber's H3 geospatial indexing library: how it divides the world into hexagonal cells at
multiple resolutions, and why hexagons are better than squares for proximity queries.

Expert Note: Learn about the two-sided marketplace dynamics: how Uber balances driver and rider
supply/demand, the role of surge pricing in incentivizing driver supply, and the long-term effects on driver
behavior.
Expert Note: Understand Uber's OSRM deployment: how they maintain a global road network graph, how
they incorporate real-time traffic data, and how they handle map updates.
Expert Note: Study the CAP theorem implications for ride-sharing: why driver location uses eventual
consistency (availability over consistency) while trip assignment uses strong consistency.
Expert Note: Learn about GPS spoofing detection: how to use accelerometer and gyroscope data to
validate GPS readings, and how machine learning models detect anomalous location patterns.
